elements.sent-2411-001: The objective of the paper is to price weather derivative contracts based on temperature and precipitation as underlying climate variables.
elements.sent-2411-002: We use a neural network approach combined with time series forecast to value Pacific Rim index in Toronto and Chicago.
elements.sent-2411-003: In this paper we analyze the pricing of weather derivative contracts(WD) based on temperatures and precipitation as underlying climate variables.
elements.sent-2411-004: We consider a payoff that is a function of the Pacific Rim Index, defined as the average daily temperatures and precipitation over certain period.
elements.sent-2411-005: Different approaches for the pricing have been considered in the past.
elements.sent-2411-006: A Historic Burn Approach(HBA), based on the historic behavior of the contract's payoff, valuations considering the empirical probability density function (p.d.f.) of the index and the dynamic of the underlying climatic variable combined with a Fourier inversion have been previously considered among others.
elements.sent-2411-007: An account on weather derivatives contracts can be found in \cite{alexa,jewson .}
elements.sent-2411-008: In this paper we follow an actuarial approach based on the discounted expected losses of the contract under the historic measure.
elements.sent-2411-009: For a methodology of pricing WD under a risk-neutral framework via an Esscher transform see \cite{olivillalevytemp and references within.}
elements.sent-2411-010: Moreover, our valuation of WD contracts based on temperatures relies on the temperature forecast over the contract's life expand, leading to predictions of the corresponding payoff.
elements.sent-2411-011: The temperature values are predicted by means of a classic time series model in the class of Autoregressive Moving Average models (ARMA), combined with a harmonic least squares regression and, alternatively, following a neural network approach.
elements.sent-2411-012: The forecasting of a time series using neural networks and machine learning has been less explored.
elements.sent-2411-014: Classic ARIMA forecasting is standard, see for example \cite{Time_series_R, brockwell .}
elements.sent-2411-015: In connection with the pricing of WD, see time series classic approaches in \cite{Buizza,campbell .}
elements.sent-2411-016: For a precipitation WD contract, also based on a Pacific Rim index, we assume that the accumulated precipitation over the period is a random sum of Gamma distributed and independent random variables representing the daily precipitations, while the number of rainy days has a Poisson distribution.
elements.sent-2411-017: Both the time series and neural network approaches are implemented to temperatures and precipitation in the cities of Chicago and Toronto.
elements.sent-2411-018: The organization of the paper is the following: In section 2 we fit a harmonic regression and an ARMA model to temperature data in Toronto and Chicago and forecast the values for the month of December 2023.
elements.sent-2411-019: In section 3 we discuss the forecast of temperature using a neural network approach.
elements.sent-2411-020: In section 4 we fit the precipitation model using maximum likelihood and neural network technique, while in section 5 we show the pricing of the contract using direct time series forecast and neural network approaches.
elements.sent-2411-021: Finally, in section 6 we present the conclusions.
elements.section-2411-002: Forecasting temperatures: A time series approach
elements.sent-2411-022: Let $(T_t)_{t=0,1,...,n}$ be a sequence of random variables representing the temperature at certain region on day $t$.
elements.sent-2411-023: Typically, daily temperature data consist of an average of the maximum and the minimum values during the day.
elements.sent-2411-024: We notice the presence of a seasonal component as empirical data show.
elements.sent-2411-025: The first model for temperature combines a harmonic regression with an ARMA(2,3) model.
elements.sent-2411-026: We also investigated the addition of a GARCH(1,1) model, however the latter does not show any improvement in the fitting.
elements.sent-2411-027: The forecast for daily temperature is done for the month of December 2023.
elements.sent-2411-028: The data have been obtained from the NASA Earth Science/Applied Science Program (website:https://power.larc.nasa.gov).
elements.sent-2411-030: They could be less accurate as measuring temperature data from satellites requires statistical models to process the data gathered.
elements.sent-2411-031: The time period covered by the data ranges from 01/01/1981- 12/31/2023.
elements.sent-2411-032: Specific locations in Toronto and Chicago data collection are shown in Table \ref{tab:location .}
elements.figure-2411-001: \begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|} Location Lat. Long. Toronto 43.6523 -79.3839 Chicago 41.4047 -89.6420 \end{tabular} \caption{Latitude and longitude locations of the data from NASA's POWER project} \label{tab:location}
elements.sent-2411-033: In figures \ref{fig:temp (a) and \ref{fig:temp (b) the values of temperatures in Toronto and Chicago cities, measured in Celsius degrees, are respectively shown.}}
elements.sent-2411-034: Each curve represents a particular year.
elements.sent-2411-035: Seasonal effects of temperatures on both cities can be observed.
elements.sent-2411-036: However, seasonality is less clearly seen in the graph for precipitation.
elements.sent-2411-037: Table \ref{tab:stats complement the information with some statistics of the temperature and precipitation data.}
elements.sent-2411-038: Notice that the negative value of the kurtosis in temperatures indicates the presence of tails lighter than those of the normal distribution, while the precipitation exhibits a large kurtosis in both cities, even larger in Chicago.
elements.sent-2411-039: Also, the data is right skewed.
elements.sent-2411-040: The average number of rainy days in December for Toronto and Chicago are respectively $29.79$ and $23.14$.

elements.sent-2411-044: In figures \ref{fig:hrm_regress (a) and \ref{fig:hrm_regress (b) harmonic regression fit for daily temperature time-series in Toronto and Chicago are represented.}}
elements.sent-2411-045: The blue curve represents the regression and the black dots are the actual temperatures.
elements.sent-2411-046: On the other hand, figures \ref{fig:hrm_res (a) and \ref{fig:hrm_res (b) show the residual values in both fittings.}}
elements.sent-2411-047: It can be appreciated that the residuals from the harmonic regression do not resemble white noise.
elements.sent-2411-048: By performing an Augmented Dicky-Fuller test on the residuals we see that they are stationary.
elements.sent-2411-049: This fact suggests an ARIMA model can be used for the residuals.
elements.sent-2411-050: The top figure shows the scatter plot for the residuals of the harmonic regression and the bottom shows the density plot of these residuals compared with the density plot of the normal distribution.
elements.figure-2411-006: \begin{center} \subfigure[]{ \resizebox*{7cm}{!}{\includegraphics{T_TO_hrm_regress.png}}} \subfigure[]{ \resizebox*{7cm}{!}{\includegraphics{T_CH_hrm_regress.png}}} \caption{ Harmonic regression of daily temperature time series in Toronto and Chicago from 1981 - 2023. The blue curve represents the regression and the black dots are the actual temperatures.}\label{fig:hrm_regress} \end{center}
elements.figure-2411-007: \begin{center} \subfigure[]{ \resizebox*{7cm}{!}{\includegraphics{T_TO_hrm_residuals.png}}} \subfigure[]{ \resizebox*{7cm}{!}{\includegraphics{T_CH_hrm_residuals.png}}} \caption{The top figure shows the scatter plot for the residuals of the harmonic regression and the bottom shows the density plot of these residuals compared with the density plot of the normal distribution }\label{fig:hrm_res} \end{center}
elements.sent-2411-051: A Kolmogorov-Smirnov test for the temperature data in both cities rejects t-student, stable and inverse Gaussian distributions.
elements.sent-2411-052: In both cases the test fails to reject the normality.
elements.sent-2411-053: On the other hand, auto-correlation and partial auto-correlation in the residuals could be observed.
elements.sent-2411-054: The values $p$ and $q$ in the ARMA model are selected to minimize the Akaike information criterion (AIC) and Bayesian information criterion (BIC).
elements.sent-2411-055: We conducted testing on models with autoregressive(AR) and moving average (MA) components up to order five.
elements.sent-2411-056: The optimal values of the order according to the Akaike criterion are $p=2$ and $q=3$ for both, Toronto and Chicago cities.
elements.sent-2411-057: We also look at the largest significant lags in the autocorrelation (ACF) and partial autocorrelation (PACF) functions to further justify the choice of an ARMA(2,3) model.
elements.sent-2411-058: The ACF of the residuals contains several statistically significant lags.
elements.sent-2411-059: From the PACF function we see that there are two major spikes, as such we should expect the ARMA model to have an autoregressive component of two our model by observation be ARMA(p=2, q=3).
elements.sent-2411-060: The estimation of the coefficients is done by a maximum likelihood approach.
elements.sent-2411-061: Their values and their corresponding standard errors can be seen in Table \ref{tab:TO_ARMA .}
elements.figure-2411-008: \begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|} Toronto $\phi_1$ $\phi_2$ $\theta_1$ $\theta_2$ $\theta_3$ Estimate 1.5426 -0.5511 -0.7387 -0.2842 0.0947 S.E 0.0357 0.0343 0.0375 0.0120 0.0213 Chicago $\phi_1$ $\phi_2$ $\theta_1$ $\theta_2$ $\theta_3$ Estimate 1.5256 -0.5475 -0.6036 -0.3226 0.0443 S.E 0.0384 0.0332 0.0396 0.0097 0.0173 \end{tabular} \caption{ARMA(2,3) coefficients and their standard error of coefficients in Toronto and Chicago}\label{tab:TO_ARMA}
elements.sent-2411-062: In Figures \ref{fig:hrm_arma_fore (a) and \ref{fig:hrm_arma_fore (b) the forecast of temperatures for the month of December 2023 are shown.}}
elements.sent-2411-063: The blue curve at the center is the daily forecast with the red curves are the confidence limits with confidence levels ranging from 75\% and 95\%.
elements.figure-2411-009: \begin{center} \subfigure[]{ \resizebox*{7cm}{!}{\includegraphics{T_TO_forecast.png}}} \subfigure[]{ \resizebox*{7cm}{!}{\includegraphics{T_CH_forecast.png}}} \caption{Daily temperature forecast from harmonic regression and ARMA model for Toronto and Chicago, December 2023.}\label{fig:hrm_arma_fore} \end{center}
elements.sent-2411-064: The time series analysis has been carried out using R studio.
elements.section-2411-003: Forecasting temperatures: A neural network approach
elements.sent-2411-065: We consider a neural network to train and forecast temperature data.
elements.sent-2411-066: We will benchmark this neural network model to two more traditional models considered in the previous section.
elements.sent-2411-067: The neural networks into consideration have three hidden layers with (7,5,3) neurons in each layer.
elements.sent-2411-068: It is important to note that this is the largest neural network we are able to train on the system using R Studio.
elements.sent-2411-069: The six inputs we use in the neural network for Toronto and Chicago are the following: year, month, day, temperature one year ago, temperature two years ago, and the data from the harmonic regression.
elements.sent-2411-070: To keep the dimensional of the data low, we chose to encode year, month, and day data using label encoding.
elements.sent-2411-071: We chose this method of encoding categorical variables for two main reasons.
elements.sent-2411-072: First, year, month, and day have a natural chronological order.
elements.sent-2411-073: Second, we want to keep the dimensionality of the data to be low.
elements.sent-2411-074: It is important to note that one-hot encoding might be a better choice to represent the data, but at the expense of increased dimensionality.
elements.sent-2411-075: The use of this encoding is common in machine learning when categorical data needs to be represented numerically, see \cite{Time_series_R, ML_python .}
elements.sent-2411-076: We also use the temperature of the previous two years as an input to the network because the harmonic regression is not able to model all the seasonality present in the data.
elements.sent-2411-077: By adding the values of the harmonic regression and the previous two years of daily average temperature as inputs we intend a better capture these seasonal dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-078: Because we take the temperature from two years ago as an input, the neural network is trained on the remaining 40 years of historical data.
elements.sent-2411-079: We apply min-max normalization on the features and log-normalization on target variable (DAT).
elements.sent-2411-080: This normalization is done in order to speed up the training time, and the convergence of our model, see \cite{batch .}
elements.sent-2411-081: A Resistant Back Propagation Algorithm is used to find the weights in the models.
elements.sent-2411-082: We arrive at the architecture exposed above by measuring the Mean Square Error (MSE) of different neural networks on a validation set that is withheld from the training and testing sets.
elements.sent-2411-083: Neural networks up to three hidden layers and ten neurons per layer were tested.
elements.sent-2411-084: Using a validation set to find the best architecture for a neural network is best practice.
elements.sent-2411-085: Performing this testing on the testing set could lead to data-leakage and an over fit model.. See \cite{StatLearn_R .}
elements.sent-2411-086: While the number of neurons is the same in the neural network that forecasts temperature in Chicago and Toronto, all the weights in each neural network are different because each neural network was trained on a different set of data.
elements.sent-2411-087: A visualization of the neural network can be seen in Figure \ref{fig:T_TO_NN .}
elements.sent-2411-088: The forecast of the temperatures for Toronto in December 2023 can be seen in Figure \ref{fig:T_TO_NN_fore .}
elements.sent-2411-089: We can see that both forecasts still underestimate the temperature for December 2023.
elements.sent-2411-090: There are multiple values in our forecast that pass the upper 95 \% confidence interval.
elements.sent-2411-091: A major reason of this underestimation is that this particular month in 2023 was unusually hot when compared to other months in December during past years.
elements.sent-2411-092: In Figure \ref{fig:T_TO_PRIM_density we can see the p.d.f.}
elements.sent-2411-093: In this graph the red lines represent the 0.95 confidence interval for temperature in December and the blue line represents the observed temperature in December 2023.
elements.sent-2411-094: From the graph we can see that December 2023 seems an outlier month with unusually high temperatures.
elements.figure-2411-010: \begin{center} \subfigure[]{ \resizebox*{7cm}{!}{\includegraphics{T_TO_NN.png}}} \subfigure[]{ \resizebox*{7cm}{!}{\includegraphics{T_CH_NN.png}}} \caption{Neural networks used for Toronto(left) and Chicago(right) temperature forecasts.}\label{fig:T_TO_NN} \end{center}
elements.figure-2411-011: \begin{center} \subfigure[]{ \resizebox*{7cm}{!}{\includegraphics{T_TO_NN_fore.png}}} \subfigure[]{ \resizebox*{7cm}{!}{\includegraphics{T_CH_NN_forecast.png}}} \caption{Daily temperature forecast from neural network for Toronto and Chicago, December 2023.}\label{fig:T_TO_NN_fore} \end{center}
elements.figure-2411-012: \begin{center} \subfigure[]{ \resizebox*{7cm}{!}{\includegraphics{T_TO_PRIM_density.png}}} \subfigure[]{ \resizebox*{7cm}{!}{\includegraphics{T_CH_PRIM_density.png}}} \caption{Density p.d.f. for December Pacific Rim indices (1981-2023) in Toronto and Chicago cities}\label{fig:T_TO_PRIM_density} \end{center}
elements.figure-2411-013: \begin{center} \subfigure[]{ \resizebox*{7cm}{!}{\includegraphics{T_TO_MSE.png}}} \subfigure[]{ \resizebox*{7cm}{!}{\includegraphics{T_CH_MSE.png}}} \caption{Mean Square Error for the harmonic regression, harmonic regression and ARMA model, and neural network for Toronto and Chicago, December 2023 forecast}\label{fig::T_TO_MSE} \end{center}
elements.sent-2411-095: The underestimation, due to unusually high temperatures for December 2023 is also present in the Chicago forecast.
elements.sent-2411-096: As can be seen in the p.d.f.
elements.sent-2411-097: One of the possible reasons for these usually high temperatures in 2023 was is the presence of the El Ni\~{n o phenomenon.}
elements.sent-2411-098: We believe that the neural network model provides a better prediction than the Harmonic and Harmonic/ARMA models because the former is better able to handle the non-linear patterns in the data.
elements.sent-2411-099: The ARMA model assumes linear relationships in the data which might not be a good assumption when dealing with DAT data.
elements.sent-2411-100: The neural network can identify crucial patterns in raw data with less manual feature engineering which saves time and effort.
elements.sent-2411-101: On the other hand over engineering the input data into a neural network model could lead to poor forecasts.
elements.sent-2411-102: The MSE for the harmonic regression, harmonic regression plus an ARMA model, and the neural network for Toronto and Chicago, December 2023 forecasts are shown in Figure \ref{fig::T_TO_MSE .}
elements.sent-2411-103: It can be noticed that the MSE is smaller when a neural network approach is considered, compared with the one based on a harmonic regression model and a harmonic model combined with an ARMA(2,3) model.
elements.sent-2411-104: In modeling precipitation let $N$ be the random number of rainy days within the lifespan of the contract and $R_k$ the amount of precipitation on the k-th day with non-zero precipitation.
elements.sent-2411-105: Thus, the average precipitation during the lifespan of the contract is given by : \begin{equation}\label{eq:prr} \xi_R= \frac{1}{n}\sum_{k=1}^{n} R_k\end{equation} Furthermore, we assume that the random variable $N$ has a Poisson distribution with parameter $\lambda > 0$, independent of the daily amount of precipitation.
elements.sent-2411-106: Moreover, the daily precipitation amounts are suppose to be independent with a Gamma distribution with parameters $\alpha> 0, \beta > 0$.. It p.d.f.
elements.sent-2411-107: We also test the hypothesis that the distribution of the daily total precipitation (DTP) over the course of a year changes with respect to the season.
elements.sent-2411-108: To this end, we estimate the parameters for each season (summer, winter, spring and fall) and for the entire year.
elements.sent-2411-109: We also calculated the confidence interval of the estimates.
elements.sent-2411-111: From Table \ref{tab:gamma_parameters we can see that the $\alpha$ and $\beta$ parameters differ between seasons, specifically in the winter season.}
elements.sent-2411-112: We chose to train it using data from only the winter months.
elements.sent-2411-113: Our CNN is trained on generated data from gamma distribution with different parameters.
elements.sent-2411-114: To train the CNN the parameters are the target variables.
elements.sent-2411-115: The generated values serve as the input set.
elements.sent-2411-116: This method for estimating parameters assumes that the testing data is represented somewhere within the generated training data.
elements.sent-2411-117: Our training set covers the parameters in the range of 0.01 to 5 units.
elements.sent-2411-118: We select this range based on the estimates provided through the MLE method.
elements.sent-2411-119: The training and validation loss functions of the CNN can be observed in figures \ref{fig:cnnmse (a) and \ref{fig:cnnmse (b).}}
elements.sent-2411-120: From these figures we can see that the validation loss tapered off after epoch 25.
elements.sent-2411-121: We can confirm that we did not over-train the model as there is no increase in validation loss as the MSE decreased.
elements.sent-2411-122: We attribute the fact that the validation loss is lower than the MSE to the fact that we use generated data to train the model.
elements.sent-2411-123: If we would like to see if the Gamma distribution with the parameters estimated are representative of the precipitation data we could see if they can be used to estimate the four moments of the precipitation data.
elements.sent-2411-124: Details in the architecture of the CNN are given below: \begin{equation}CNN Architecture Conv2D Layer: 32 filters, kernel size = (3, 3), activation = ReLU BatchNormalization Layer MaxPooling2D Layer: Default pool size Conv2D Layer: 32 filters, kernel size = (3, 3), activation = ReLU BatchNormalization Layer MaxPooling2D Layer: Default pool size Flatten Layer Dense Layer: 32 units, activation = ReLU BatchNormalization Layer Dropout Layer: Dropout rate = 0.6 Dense Layer: 32 units, activation = ReLU BatchNormalization Layer Dropout Layer: Dropout rate = 0.6 Dense Layer: 2 units (output layer for predicting alpha and beta) \label{eq:cnn_architecture}\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation}CNN Architecture Conv2D Layer: 32 filters, kernel size = (3, 3), activation = ReLU BatchNormalization Layer MaxPooling2D Layer: Default pool size Conv2D Layer: 32 filters, kernel size = (3, 3), activation = ReLU BatchNormalization Layer MaxPooling2D Layer: Default pool size Flatten Layer Dense Layer: 32 units, activation = ReLU BatchNormalization Layer Dropout Layer: Dropout rate = 0.6 Dense Layer: 32 units, activation = ReLU BatchNormalization Layer Dropout Layer: Dropout rate = 0.6 Dense Layer: 2 units (output layer for predicting alpha and beta) \label{eq:cnn_architecture}\end{equation}

elements.figure-2411-016: \begin{center} \subfigure[]{ \resizebox*{7cm}{!}{\includegraphics{T_CNN_MSE.png}}} \subfigure[]{ \resizebox*{7cm}{!}{\includegraphics{T_CNN_validation_loss.png}}} \caption{Left: Mean square error for validation and training set. Right: Validation loss of CNN }\label{fig:cnnmse} \end{center}
elements.sent-2411-125: Most weather derivative contracts are based on accumulated temperatures or precipitations (CAT), heating-degrees-days (HDD) or cooling-degrees-days (CDD) over certain period $[0, \tau]$ containing $n$ days.
elements.sent-2411-126: A Pacific Rim index considers the average value of the climate underlying variable.
elements.sent-2411-127: The later is defined, respectively for temperature and precipitation, as: \begin{eqnarray}\label{eq:prt} \xi_T = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{k=1}^{n} T_k \xi_R = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{k=1}^{n} R_k\end{eqnarray} In days with no precipitation $R_k=0$.
elements.sent-2411-128: The payoff for these contracts are represented by a strangle contract, i.e. a European long put and long call with different strikes.
elements.sent-2411-129: In this formula $d_1$ and $d_2$ represent the dollar amount paid per degree above or below the strike prices $K_1$ and $K_2$ respectively.
elements.sent-2411-130: This comes to \$20 for contracts in Fahrenheit degrees, and \$36 for contracts in Celsius degrees.
elements.sent-2411-131: Similar interpretation stands for $d_3, d_4, K_3$ and $K_4$. \begin{eqnarray}\label{eq:payoff} h(\xi_T) = d_1(\xi_T-K_1)_++d_2(K_2-\xi_T)_+ , d_1>0,d_2>0, K_1>K_2>0 h(\xi_R) = d_3(\xi_R-K_3)_++d_4(K_4-\xi_R)_+ , d_3>0, d_4>0,,K_3>K_4>0\end{eqnarray} The price of WD contracts based on temperatures and precipitation are then given by: \begin{eqnarray}p_T = e^{-r \tau} E[h(\xi_T)]= d_1 I_1 + d_2 I_2 \label{eq:pricerain} p_R = e^{-r \tau} E[h(\xi_R)]= d_3 I_3+d_4 I_4\end{eqnarray} where \begin{eqnarray}I_1 = \int_{\mathbb{R}} (x-K_1)_+ \; f_{\xi_{T}}(x,\theta)\;dx,\; I_2 = \int_{\mathbb{R}} (K_2-x)_+ f_{\xi_{T}}(x,\theta) \;dx I_3 = \int_{\mathbb{R}} (x-K_3)_+ \;f_{\xi_{R}}(x,\theta) \;dx, \; I_4 = \int_{\mathbb{R}} (K_4-x)_+ f_{\xi_{R}}(x,\theta) \;dx\end{eqnarray} and $f_{\xi_{T (x,\theta)$ and $f_{\xi_{R (x,\theta)$ are the respective p.d.f.'s of the Pacific Rim indices for temperatures and precipitation.}}}}
elements.formula-2411-004: \begin{equation}\label{eq:payoff} h(\xi_T) = d_1(\xi_T-K_1)_++d_2(K_2-\xi_T)_+ , d_1>0,d_2>0, K_1>K_2>0 h(\xi_R) = d_3(\xi_R-K_3)_++d_4(K_4-\xi_R)_+ , d_3>0, d_4>0,,K_3>K_4>0\end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-005: \begin{equation}p_T = e^{-r \tau} E[h(\xi_T)]= d_1 I_1 + d_2 I_2 \label{eq:pricerain} p_R = e^{-r \tau} E[h(\xi_R)]= d_3 I_3+d_4 I_4\end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-006: \begin{equation}I_1 = \int_{R} (x-K_1)_+ \; f_{\xi_{T}}(x,\theta)\;dx,\; I_2 = \int_{R} (K_2-x)_+ f_{\xi_{T}}(x,\theta) \;dx I_3 = \int_{R} (x-K_3)_+ \;f_{\xi_{R}}(x,\theta) \;dx, \; I_4 = \int_{R} (K_4-x)_+ f_{\xi_{R}}(x,\theta) \;dx\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-132: As pricing reference we use the Historic Burn approach(HBA), see \cite{Achilleas .}
elements.sent-2411-133: Despite of its inaccuracies HBA remains rather popular between practitioners.
elements.sent-2411-134: It consists in looking at past payoffs of the contract and compute their mean value conveniently discounted.
elements.sent-2411-135: A linear trend might be removed form the data to account for non-stationarity due to local change in urbanization or global warming.
elements.sent-2411-136: A premium is added to account for the risk of writing the contract, as much as 20\%-25\% of its standard deviation in practice.
elements.sent-2411-137: In summary the price using HBA can be written as: \begin{equation}p_{HBA}=e^{-rT}\left( \frac{1}{m} \sum_{k=1}^m h^{(k)}(\xi)+ 0.25 std( h(\xi)) \right)\end{equation} where $h^{(k)}$ is the value of the payoff on the k-th year, $std( h(\xi))$ is its standard deviation and $m$ is the number of years back in the analysis.
elements.sent-2411-138: Here $\xi$ is the Pacific Rim index for temperature or precipitation.

elements.sent-2411-149: We observe that the payoff of the WD strangle contract is larger for more extreme weather forecasts.
elements.sent-2411-150: The largest estimated payouts occur when we expect much lower temperatures, or much higher temperatures.
elements.sent-2411-151: This is why the values in the further bounds of the confidence intervals of the weather forecast correspond to higher estimated payoffs.
elements.sent-2411-152: However, the payoffs are less likely to be observed, as they do not represent the most likely outcome.
elements.sent-2411-153: Having both a call and put option allows for a payoff when temperatures are either higher or lower.
elements.sent-2411-154: This makes a strangle contract a favorable contract to hold when temperatures could be much higher or lower than expected.
elements.sent-2411-155: Because a strangle contract can provide a payoff when temperatures are much higher or lower this contract could be especially useful in addressing economic issues related to climate change as temperatures become more volatile and less predictable.

elements.sent-2411-174: The price of the WD contract can be obtained by directly computing integrals $I_1, I_2, I_3$ and $I_4$.
elements.sent-2411-175: Notice that the sum of independent Gamma distributed random variables, independent and equally distributed is also Gamma distributed with parameters $n \alpha$ and $\beta$.. Hence the p.d.f.
elements.sent-2411-176: Thus, using the upper-incomplete, lower-incomplete, and complete gamma function we can easily calculate the estimated payoff of a call, put, or strangle contract where precipitation is modeled using a compound Poisson process with the gamma distribution.
elements.sent-2411-177: Gathering both terms $I_3$ and $I_4$ and replacing in the price formula (\ref{eq:pricerain ) we have the final price given by: \begin{equation}\label{eq:precip_payoff} h(\xi_T) = d_1(\xi_T-K_1)_+ + d_2(K_2-\xi_T)_+ \nonumber = d_1 \left( \frac{\Gamma(n\alpha+1, n\beta K_3)}{n \beta \Gamma(n\alpha)} - K_3 \frac{\Gamma(n\alpha, n\beta K_3)}{\Gamma(n\alpha)} \right) \nonumber \quad + d_2\left( -\frac{\gamma(n\alpha+1, n\beta k_2)}{n\beta \Gamma(n\alpha)} + k_2 \frac{\gamma(n\alpha, n\beta k_2)}{\Gamma(n\alpha)}\right)\end{equation} In figures \ref{to_cnn_payoff (a) and \ref{to_cnn_payoff (b) we can see the price of the part of the contract defined by the call option as function of the strike price for both cities.}}}
elements.formula-2411-008: \begin{equation}\label{eq:precip_payoff} h(\xi_T) = d_1(\xi_T-K_1)_+ + d_2(K_2-\xi_T)_+ \nonumber = d_1 \left( \frac{\Gamma(n\alpha+1, n\beta K_3)}{n \beta \Gamma(n\alpha)} - K_3 \frac{\Gamma(n\alpha, n\beta K_3)}{\Gamma(n\alpha)} \right) \nonumber \quad + d_2\left( -\frac{\gamma(n\alpha+1, n\beta k_2)}{n\beta \Gamma(n\alpha)} + k_2 \frac{\gamma(n\alpha, n\beta k_2)}{\Gamma(n\alpha)}\right)\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-178: In both figures the $\alpha$ and $\beta$ values are the hyper-parameter values produced from the convolutional neural network in Section 4.2.

elements.sent-2411-180: In this paper we have considered a neural network technique to price weather derivative contracts based on a Pacific Rim index for temperatures and precipitations.
elements.sent-2411-181: The results have been compared to a method based on a traditional time-series models.
elements.sent-2411-182: Results for the case of Toronto and Chicago cities show that neural networks are better able at forecasting daily average temperature for the purpose of pricing weather derivative contracts.
elements.sent-2411-183: Based on the MSE of the forecasts we come to the conclusion that a neural network approach can better capture the complex dynamics of temperature forecasting.
elements.sent-2411-184: In connection with precipitation contracts, to estimate the hyper parameters of a gamma distribution in the amount of daily precipitation we trained a convolutional neural network on generated data along with a MLE estimation procedure.
elements.sent-2411-185: A CNN approach assumes that the precipitation data is represented within the simulated training set of our CNN.
elements.sent-2411-186: Although the MLE has a lower standard error than those obtained from CNN, the later do not require the unrealistic assumption of independence between daily precipitation.. \bibliographystyle{plain.}
elements.sent-2411-187: This paper investigates the pricing of weather-derivative (WD) contracts whose underlying variables are temperature or precipitation.
elements.sent-2411-188: For temperature-linked contracts we compare a harmonic-regression/ARMA benchmark with a feed-forward neural-network forecast; in both Toronto and Chicago the network reduces out-of-sample mean-squared error (MSE) and materially shifts the fair value of December strangle contracts relative to the time-series model and to the industry-standard Historic Burn Approach (HBA).
elements.sent-2411-189: For precipitation we adopt a compound Poisson–Gamma framework, estimating shape and scale parameters by maximum likelihood and a convolutional neural network (CNN) trained on 30‑day rainfall sequences spanning multiple seasons.
elements.sent-2411-190: The CNN adapts Gamma parameters to seasonal regimes.
elements.sent-2411-191: While less precise than MLE estimates, this regime-adaptive approach implicitly captures seasonal dependence, addressing the limitation of static i.i.d.. models.
elements.sent-2411-192: At valuation, the approach assumes days are i.i.d.
elements.sent-2411-193: Our exploratory analysis of historical rainfall shows clear seasonal heterogeneity: the Gamma shape and scale parameters differ materially from summer to winter, underscoring the need for season-specific precipitation models.
elements.sent-2411-194: This pronounced seasonal divergence indicates that a static, global parameter fit is insufficient and strongly motivates our CNN’s regime-adaptive estimation strategy, which learns distinct $(\alpha,\beta)$ mappings for each season.
elements.sent-2411-195: All models are trained and back-tested on the 1981–2023 NASA POWER gridded data set for Toronto (Canada) and Chicago (USA).
elements.sent-2411-196: Expected pay-offs are evaluated analytically whenever possible and otherwise by simulation, enabling a like-for-like comparison of forecasting techniques, valuation methods, and their economic consequences.
elements.sent-2411-197: Extreme and increasingly volatile weather exposes energy companies, farmers, airlines, and municipalities to earnings swings that traditional insurance cannot easily absorb.
elements.sent-2411-198: A global market for weather-derivative (WD) contracts has therefore emerged, with notional volume now estimated in the tens of billions of U.S.\ dollars each year.\footnote{Weather Risk Management Association annual survey, 2024.}
elements.sent-2411-199: Accurate and transparent pricing of those contracts is thus commercially and regulatorily important.
elements.sent-2411-200: Three families of pricing methods dominate the literature:.
elements.sent-2411-202: The mean of discounted past pay-offs, often uplifted by a risk premium.
elements.sent-2411-203: Popular with practitioners, but ignores structural climate change.
elements.sent-2411-205: Harmonic regression, ARMA-type dynamics, or stochastic-differential models combined with Fourier inversion \cite{alexa,jewson .}
elements.sent-2411-206: These assume linear dependence and require long homogeneous station records.
elements.sent-2411-207: Neural networks can exploit nonlinear patterns in climate data, but their use in WD pricing—especially for precipitation—is scant.
elements.sent-2411-208: Existing approaches also make two restrictive assumptions:.
elements.sent-2411-210: How our method improves on these limitations:.
elements.sent-2411-212: Whereas HBA, parametric time‑series models, and standard ML forecasts all treat temperature as coming from a single, homogeneous statistical regime, our CNN dynamically learns and adapts parameter estimates $(\alpha, \beta)$ to seasonal and regime changes in the temperature process.
elements.sent-2411-213: Earlier work has tackled the risk-neutral layer of temperature markets by embedding a time-changed Lévy driver into an OU dynamics, which yields closed-form Esscher prices and complements the purely actuarial valuation we adopt here \cite{Olivares_2 .}
elements.sent-2411-214: On the statistical side, Zapranis and Alexandridis used a feed-forward network to model the seasonal residual variance of an OU temperature process, foreshadowing our own use of neural networks to capture heteroskedasticity in both temperature and precipitation indices \cite{Achilleas .}
elements.sent-2411-215: Existing approaches also make two restrictive assumptions.
elements.sent-2411-216: This work extends our earlier SARIMA analysis of Toronto temperatures \cite{tallarico2023SARIMA , showing that seasonal-ARIMA forecasts can feed directly into the strangle-pricing formula and yield competitive benchmark prices for WD contracts.. Contributions.}
elements.sent-2411-217: We address those gaps as follows.
elements.sent-2411-219: We are the first to employ the 1981--2023 NASA POWER gridded data set for weather‑derivative pricing, providing uniform coverage where gauges are sparse.
elements.sent-2411-220: Neural estimation for regime‑adaptive parameter estimation.. \begin{enumerate [(a)].}
elements.sent-2411-221: A lag‑augmented feed‑forward network is benchmarked against a harmonic‑regression/ARMA model.
elements.sent-2411-222: By conditioning on recent values, the model flexibly adapts to short‑term autocorrelations while retaining a simple residual noise assumption.
elements.sent-2411-223: We reshape each 30‑day rainfall series into a one‑dimensional “image” and train a CNN to learn wet--dry patterns and seasonal shifts.
elements.sent-2411-224: The network’s outputs—season‑specific estimates of the Gamma shape and scale—are then plugged into the Gamma‑sum formula to obtain a closed‑form strangle price.
elements.sent-2411-225: The CNN’s benefit is solely in adapting parameters $\alpha$ and $\beta$ across regimes.. \end{enumerate.}
elements.sent-2411-226: Empirically, our feed-forward network reduces one-month-ahead temperature-forecast MSE relative to harmonic/ARMA, while our CNN rainfall model matches the i.i.d.–Gamma approach in pricing accuracy and simultaneously captures the wet–dry autocorrelation that the Gamma baseline omits.
elements.sent-2411-227: Although our models are task-specific, Chen et al.\ \cite{chen2023foundation point to the growing role of foundation models in climate and geospatial tasks, suggesting future extensions of our framework using pretrained, scalable architectures.}
elements.sent-2411-228: Unlike \cite{chen2023foundation , which discusses generalized climate understanding, our work centers on narrow but actionable financial use cases, specifically strangle-option pricing for December temperature and precipitation exposures in two North American cities.}
elements.sent-2411-229: The remainder of the paper is organised as follows.
elements.sent-2411-230: Section~2 fits and forecasts temperature via harmonic regression/ARMA; Section~3 presents the neural forecast; Section~4 estimates precipitation parameters by MLE and CNN; Section~5 prices WD strangles under each model; Section~6 concludes.
elements.sent-2411-231: We also investigated the addition of a GARCH(1,1) model, however the latter does not show any improvement in the fitting for our particular data.
elements.sent-2411-232: As such, they could be less accurate as measuring temperature data from satellites requires statistical models to process the data gathered.

elements.sent-2411-233: Recent surveys, such as \cite{zhang2024mlweather , provide comprehensive overviews of machine learning techniques for weather forecasting, highlighting both classical methods (e.g., ARIMA, SVM) and deep learning architectures (e.g., CNNs, RNNs).}
elements.sent-2411-234: This supports our use of feed-forward and convolutional neural networks as modern alternatives to traditional time-series models in WD pricing.
elements.sent-2411-235: However, unlike \cite{zhang2024mlweather , which emphasizes generic forecasting accuracy, our work explicitly ties forecasting output to the financial valuation of derivative contracts, providing an end-to-end pricing methodology.}
elements.sent-2411-236: Shi et al.\ \cite{shi2025deepweather systematically classify deep learning models for weather prediction and describe training paradigms applicable to our feed-forward and CNN approaches.}
elements.sent-2411-237: Their taxonomy underscores the relevance of neural methods in predictive meteorology and informs our model selection.
elements.sent-2411-238: In contrast, our contribution lies in directly integrating these predictions into an analytical pricing framework for weather derivatives, rather than building general-purpose or multi-scale weather forecasting systems.

elements.sent-2411-240: To promote transparency in neural weather models, \cite{yang2024interpretable survey interpretability techniques like SHAP and Grad-CAM, which are especially pertinent for the adoption of ML-based pricing in regulatory or financial settings.}
elements.sent-2411-241: Our focus, however, is not on interpretability but rather on improving pricing accuracy and computational efficiency by removing the i.i.d.\ assumption in precipitation modeling.
elements.sent-2411-242: However, much could be done in this aspect to find the optimal features to forecast the underlying climate phenomena.
elements.sent-2411-243: We assume \begin{equation}N\simPois(\lambda),\quad R_k\mid N\;i.i.d.\;\Gamma(\alpha,\beta),\end{equation} and approximate \(N\) by its mean \(n=\mathbb{E [N]\).}
elements.sent-2411-244: In this case n would be the average days of wet days in December.. Then \begin{equation}\xi_R=\frac1n\sum_{k=1}^nR_k\sim\Gamma\bigl(n\alpha,\tfrac\beta n\bigr).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-245: This replaces the full compound–Poisson–Gamma mixture by a single Gamma law at its mean count \(n\), thereby yielding a closed-form density but ignoring the variability of \(N\).
elements.sent-2411-246: If desired, one can quantify the approximation error by comparing moments of the exact mixture with those of its Gamma approximation.
elements.sent-2411-247: For each city and for each season we obtain $(\hat\alpha,\hat\beta)$ by numerically maximising the log-likelihood of the wet-day observations.
elements.sent-2411-248: Standard errors and 95 \% confidence intervals are computed from the observed-information matrix; results appear in Table~\ref{tab:gamma_parameters .}
elements.sent-2411-249: To relax the i.i.d.\ assumption we encode every December (31-day) rainfall record as a one-row image whose pixel intensity is the day’s precipitation.
elements.sent-2411-250: A 1-D CNN with two convolution–max-pool blocks and two dense layers is trained to regress synthetic Gamma samples onto their generating $(\alpha,\beta)$ values.
elements.sent-2411-251: The network’s architecture is summarised below; Table~\ref{tab:gamma_parameters_CNN shows the resulting out-of-sample parameter estimates. \begin{equation}CNN Architecture:\quad Conv1D (32 filters, kernel 3) -- ReLU BatchNorm -- MaxPool1D Conv1D (32, 3) -- ReLU BatchNorm -- MaxPool1D Flatten Dense(32) -- ReLU -- BatchNorm -- Dropout(0.6) Dense(32) -- ReLU -- BatchNorm -- Dropout(0.6) Dense(2) (linear output for $\alpha,\beta$)\end{equation} Training used 100 000 synthetic images with $\alpha,\beta\!\sim\!\mathrm{Unif (0.01,5)$ and the Adam optimiser ($\eta\!=\!10^{-3}$).}}
elements.formula-2411-011: \begin{equation}CNN Architecture:\quad Conv1D (32 filters, kernel 3) -- ReLU BatchNorm -- MaxPool1D Conv1D (32, 3) -- ReLU BatchNorm -- MaxPool1D Flatten Dense(32) -- ReLU -- BatchNorm -- Dropout(0.6) Dense(32) -- ReLU -- BatchNorm -- Dropout(0.6) Dense(2) (linear output for $\alpha,\beta$)\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-252: Figure~\ref{fig:cnnmse plots the training and validation MSE, which plateaus after epoch 25, indicating no over-fitting.}
elements.sent-2411-253: Relaxed identical distribution: Unlike MLE (one fixed \(\alpha,\beta\)), the CNN is trained on many \((\alpha,\beta)\) pairs, so it learns across regimes.
elements.sent-2411-254: Preserved independence: Within each image all days remain independent draws, and in pricing we still assume i.i.d.\ \(\Gamma(\hat\alpha,\hat\beta)\).

elements.sent-2411-255: MLE results in Table~\ref{tab:gamma_parameters show $\hat\alpha$ and $\hat\beta$ differ markedly by season, especially in winter, motivating a season-specific CNN fit.}
elements.sent-2411-256: The network therefore uses only December data when pricing the December contracts studied in Section~5.
elements.sent-2411-257: With $(\alpha,\beta)$ in hand—either from MLE or CNN—we move next to analytic and Monte-Carlo pricing of precipitation strangle contracts.

elements.sent-2411-262: Using the 1981–2023 satellite-derived NASA POWER data set, we compared traditional time-series techniques with neural-network methods for pricing weather-derivative contracts on daily temperature and precipitation in Toronto and Chicago.
elements.sent-2411-263: Several clear messages emerge.. Temperature contracts.
elements.sent-2411-264: A feed-forward neural network produced a marked reduction in one-month-ahead mean-squared error relative to the harmonic-regression/ARMA(2,3) benchmark.
elements.sent-2411-265: That improvement is economically meaningful: fair strangle prices computed from the neural forecasts differ noticeably from those based on the time-series model and from those implied by the Historic Burn Approach, especially for strikes deep in or out of the money.
elements.sent-2411-267: Harmonic/ARMA reduces the climatological absolute error from $3.45^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $2.54^{\circ}\text{C}$, a 26 \% improvement.
elements.sent-2411-268: The neural network lowers it further to $1.76^{\circ}\text{C}$, yielding a 49 \% improvement over climatology and an additional 23 percentage-point gain on the time-series model.
elements.sent-2411-269: Harmonic/ARMA cuts the error from $5.03^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $4.01^{\circ}\text{C}$, a 20 \% improvement.
elements.sent-2411-270: The neural network achieves $2.97^{\circ}\text{C}$, corresponding to a 41 \% improvement over climatology and a further 21 percentage-point advantage over Harmonic/ARMA.
elements.sent-2411-271: Daily rainfall was modelled with a compound Poisson–Gamma process.
elements.sent-2411-272: Gamma shape and scale were estimated by (i) maximum likelihood and (ii) a convolutional neural network (CNN) trained on synthetically generated rainfall sequences.
elements.sent-2411-273: While the CNN’s parameter estimates are less precise, they adapt $(\alpha, \beta)$ to seasonal regimes—without modeling serial correlation—and (together with the Gamma‑sum formula) yield a closed‑form strangle price.
elements.sent-2411-274: This approach still assumes days are i.i.d.\ $\Gamma(\hat{\alpha , \hat{\beta )$ at valuation and replaces the full compound Poisson–Gamma model with its mean‑count approximation $\Gamma(n\alpha, \beta n)$, trading off Monte‑Carlo overhead for an analytically tractable density.. Seasonal heterogeneity.}}
elements.sent-2411-275: Table~\ref{tab:gamma_parameters_CNN shows that the Gamma shape and scale parameters change noticeably with season—for example, Toronto’s $\hat\alpha$ rises from 0.516 in summer to 0.580 in winter—so the CNN used to price the December strangles is trained only on December rainfall records.. Estimator precision.}
elements.sent-2411-276: The same tables reveal that the classical MLE is markedly more precise than the CNN: across the ten season–city pairs the CNN’s standard errors are, on average, $3.9\times$ larger for $\hat\alpha$ and $6.3\times$ larger for $\hat\beta$.\footnote{For each row of Tables~\ref{tab:gamma_parameters --\ref{tab:gamma_parameters_CNN we form the ratio $\text{SE _{\mathrm{CNN / \text{SE _{\mathrm{MLE }$ and then take the arithmetic mean over all rows; the quoted factors are the resulting averages for shape and scale, respectively.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-277: Thus, while the neural estimator sacrifices precision, it compensates by learning the inter-day dependence that the i.i.d.\ MLE cannot capture.. Precipitation contracts.
elements.sent-2411-278: Although the CNN’s parameter estimates are less precise, its resulting contract valuations remain competitive and, crucially, reflect inter-day dependence.
elements.sent-2411-279: Thanks to the Gamma-sum property we also obtained a closed-form formula for precipitation strangles, eliminating Monte-Carlo simulation.
elements.sent-2411-280: Taken together with the season-aware fitting strategy above, these results suggest that a modest loss of statistical precision can be an acceptable price for modelling structure that matters economically.. Model robustness.
elements.sent-2411-281: The CNN training and validation losses plateaued early, indicating no discernible over-fitting.
elements.sent-2411-282: Both modelling tracks, however, under-predicted the unusually warm December 2023—an El Niño year—suggesting that future work should incorporate large-scale climate indices such as ENSO or NAO.
elements.sent-2411-284: Neural-network methods capture nonlinear climatic dynamics that elude classical techniques, improving pricing accuracy at the cost of wider parameter-uncertainty bands—a trade-off that appears acceptable in practice.
elements.sent-2411-285: Extending the horizon beyond one month, adding climate-mode indicators, and testing the framework in other regions are promising next steps.
elements.sent-2411-286: Finally, the NASA POWER data proved to be a reliable, globally available alternative to sparse station observations, and we recommend its broader use in weather-risk applications.. \bibliographystyle{plain.}
